blocks.title: The Car Project — 9 days
blocks.markdown: For the next **9 days you will build a car**, then race it down the corridor. On Day 9 you will not only race — you will write a **transfer task** defending **three physics choices** you made. Every build decision this unit is a physics decision in disguise. Today is the kickoff: we walk the race corridor, meet the four safety zones, and commit a team theme.

blocks.statement: I can name the four components of a DC circuit (battery, switch, motor, wires in series) — at least by sight.
I can preview the energy chain that drives the car: chemical → electrical → mechanical-rotational → mechanical-translational.
I can explain that a car veers because of friction asymmetry, not lack of power — a geometry problem, not a strength problem.

blocks.markdown:
### Walk the corridor

Stand inside the actual race corridor. Notice how **narrow** it is. Notice the length — about **6 meters**. The corridor is not decoration; it **is the design constraint**.

Here is the thing that should worry you: a car that starts pointed just **2 cm off-true** does not stay 2 cm off. The error grows over the whole 6 m run. A tiny misalignment on build day becomes a wall-scraping disaster on race day. That is why the days ahead obsess over straight axle lines and parallel wheels.

blocks.title: Four safety zones — show, don't touch (yet)
blocks.markdown:
This room has **four hazard zones**. You will be trained on each before you use it, and every day opens with a 60-second safety beat on the relevant zone.

1. **Soldering iron** — hot enough to burn instantly; never set down off its stand.
2. **Hot glue** — the glue AND the nozzle stay dangerously hot for minutes.
3. **Drill** — eye protection on, hands clear, work clamped.
4. **Switch installation / first power-on** — live circuits; respect the loop.

Today you only **observe** these stations. Do not power anything, heat anything, or cut anything without a teacher present.

terms.definition: The source — stores chemical energy and pushes current around the loop.
The gate — opens or closes the loop to start and stop the motor.
The load — converts electrical energy into spinning (rotational) motion.
Components wired one after another in a single loop; break it anywhere and current stops everywhere.
The flow of charge around the closed loop that makes the motor turn.

blocks.title: Team roles (3–4 per team)
blocks.markdown: Pick roles now so every build day runs smoothly: **build lead** (drives construction), **safety officer** (owns the zone checklist), and **scribe** (records data and decisions in this packet). A fourth member shares the build-lead and scribe load.

blocks.prompt: Quick estimate: the race corridor is about 6 m long. If your car can cover roughly 2 m every second, about how long does one clean run take? (This sets your expectations for the race — no precise data yet, just a back-of-the-envelope estimate.)
blocks.givenHint: distance d ≈ 6 m; estimated speed v ≈ 2 m/s

blocks.instruction: THEME COMMIT — sketch your team's car concept (silhouette, colors, signature feature), then log your reasoning in the boxes below. A stranger should be able to recognize your theme from the sketch alone.

blocks.frame: Our team's theme is ___ . It fits a fast, straight-rolling race car because ___ . The part of the build I am most worried about is ___ because ___ .

blocks.prompt: Exit ticket: In one or two sentences, name your team theme AND the single biggest physical challenge you expect to face building it.
blocks.frame: Our theme is ___ ; the biggest challenge we expect is ___ .
